title: The Incarnation Is a Model for Humility
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Incarnation Is A Model For Humility (Pastor Tito Lyro).txt
This Incarnation is a Model for Christian Living.txt
outline: The incarnation as the supreme model for Christian living
Consolation in Christ, fellowship of the Spirit, and like-mindedness (vv.1-2)
Let nothing be done through selfish ambition or conceit (v.3)
Christ’s condescension: equal with God, took the form of a servant (vv.6-7)
Humbled Himself to death, even death on a cross (v.8)
Therefore God exalted Him above every name (vv.9-11)
Applying the humility of Christ to daily life
summary: Part of an Advent series on Christ’s incarnation as a model for Christian living. Expounding Philippians 2:1-11, the Christ Hymn, the sermon presents the incarnation as the supreme example of humility—Christ who was equal with God took the form of a servant, humbled Himself to death on a cross, and was therefore exalted by God. Calls believers to the same humble, other-centered mind in their daily lives.
transcript:
## The Humble Mind of Christ

During this Advent season we are considering four virtues that Scripture connects to the incarnation of our Lord Jesus Christ: humility modeled in his descent, generosity displayed in his self-giving, grace shown in his fullness, and love demonstrated in his pursuit of us.

This morning we begin with humility, drawn from Philippians 2:1–11 — what is sometimes called in Latin the *Carmen Christi*, the Hymn of Christ. Here we find the perfect portrait of the humble mind and heart to which we are called. Peter says that humility is like a garment we are to put on:

> *"Yes, all of you be submissive to one another, and be clothed with humility, for God resists the proud but gives grace to the humble."* (1 Peter 5:5)

## Putting On the Mind of Christ

Paul teaches us that humility begins with putting on the mind of Christ:

> *"Let this mind be in you which was also in Christ Jesus, who, being in the form of God, did not consider it robbery to be equal with God, but made himself of no reputation, taking the form of a bondservant, and coming in the likeness of men."* (Philippians 2:5–7)

Christ did not grasp at his rights. He did not say, "It is my right not to be limited, not to suffer, not to become like one of them." Instead, he endured the humiliation of becoming human — with all the infirmities and limitations that entails — and this had nothing to do with sin. The human existence itself is a limited existence.

Consider what his birth involved. He is the one who created the universe, who sustains all things by the word of his power — and yet he was born in a barn, laid in a feeding trough, with no witnesses except shepherds who were the lowest class in first-century Palestinian culture. Then came a life growing up under sinful parents with sinful brothers and sisters, despised and rejected by his own people — and finally dying the death of a criminal. Paul writes:

> *"And being found in appearance as a man, He humbled Himself and became obedient to the point of death, even the death of the cross."* (Philippians 2:8)

> *"Christ has redeemed us from the curse of the law, having become a curse for us, for it is written, 'Cursed is everyone who hangs on a tree.'"* (Galatians 3:13)

That is the humiliation — the humbling of the Son of God who became our Savior by dying the death of sinners, a death of separation from his Father, a death that you and I deserved.

## Esteeming Others Better Than Ourselves

How does putting on humility through the mind of Christ look in practice? Paul points us to a specific pattern:

> *"Let nothing be done through selfish ambition or conceit, but in lowliness of mind let each esteem others better than himself. Let each of you look out not only for his own interests, but also for the interests of others."* (Philippians 2:3–4)

Notice: it is not about self-esteem. It is about *others*-esteem. Paul says in Romans 12:3, "Do not think of yourself more highly than you ought to think, but think soberly." Our natural tendency is to think very highly of ourselves. Paul says: that is not the way of humility.

## Fourteen Ways to Esteem Others Better Than Yourself

**1.** Do not assume that others have the same evil motives you find in your own heart. Put the best possible interpretation on their actions. Love "believes all things" (1 Corinthians 13:7). Cultivate a predisposition to think well of others.

**2.** Look for godly qualities in others that you know you most need yourself, then seek their help in acquiring those qualities. Paul says, "Imitate me as I imitate Christ" (1 Corinthians 11:1). Find someone who is humble and grow under them.

**3.** Do not assume that your time, money, energy, thoughts, or opinions are more valuable than your neighbor's.

**4.** When making decisions, consider not only how they affect your own interests but also how they affect others — your spouse, your children, your church.

**5.** Be alert not only to your own needs but also to the needs of others. This requires knowing people — reaching out, asking how you can bless and serve them.

**6.** Honor those biblical characteristics in others that are worthy of honor. Lift up what the Bible lifts up, not what the world celebrates.

**7.** Pray for your brothers and sisters in Christ. To grow in humility, pray for the brethren.

**8.** Remind yourself often that God has given you everything you have and that he has often used others to get you where you are. No accomplishment is truly our own. Our attitude should match the servant in Luke 17:

> *"So likewise you, when you have done all those things which you are commanded, say, 'We are unprofitable servants. We have done what was our duty to do.'"* (Luke 17:10)

Thankfulness is a core element of humility. Ungrateful people are proud people.

**9.** Give your reputation to God — just as Jesus "made himself of no reputation" (Philippians 2:7). A proud person has made an idol of his own reputation; a humble person is concerned about Christ's reputation. Paul writes:

> *"Beloved, do not avenge yourselves, but rather give place to wrath; for it is written, 'Vengeance is Mine, I will repay,' says the Lord."* (Romans 12:19)

**10.** Be quick to acknowledge when you are wrong. James writes: "Confess your trespasses to one another" (James 5:16). We are often afraid that if we confess our sins to people they will find out we are not perfect. But there is no one righteous — no, not one (Romans 3:10). We only have worth because of Jesus Christ.

**11.** Forgive freely. The standard Paul gives is breathtaking: "Forgive one another, even as God in Christ forgave you" (Ephesians 4:32). God forgives completely and permanently:

> *"I will forgive their iniquity, and their sin I will remember no more."* (Jeremiah 31:34)

If you are truly in Christ, God holds nothing against you — only the righteousness of Christ. Forgiveness is fundamentally a promise, not a feeling.

**12.** Meditate on the Word of God. Psalm 119:97 says, "Oh, how I love Your law! It is my meditation all the day." Putting on the mind of Christ involves reading and thinking deeply about Scripture.

**13.** Redirect praise to God and to those who helped you get where you are. "Give honor to whom honor is due" (Romans 13:7).

**14.** Associate with the humble. Paul says, "Do not set your mind on high things, but associate with the humble" (Romans 12:16). Are you willing to develop friendships with those who can model genuine humility and who can do very little to enhance your social standing?

## The Danger of Conceit

Paul says in Philippians 2:3 that conceit — literally *kenodoxia*, "empty glory" — is not to be found among the brethren. The word is the same used in Ecclesiastes for "vanity." That empty glory looks solid but collapses beneath you like a sinkhole. Pride destroys unity in the church, the family, and every relationship. Paul's answer is clear:

> *"He who glories, let him glory in the Lord."* (1 Corinthians 1:31)

## Conclusion

Christ's way of thinking is one of self-denial, of lowering himself, of elevating others above himself, of cultivating unity by emptying himself. That is the mind we are to put on. Paul calls us to it plainly:

> *"Let this mind be in you which was also in Christ Jesus."* (Philippians 2:5)

Are you thinking in his way, or in your own way — the way of pride? May the Lord humble us that we may follow in the steps of our Savior.
